BlueMoon RCEv1.0 — Cross-Disease Clinical Applicability

Improving Clinical Trial Outcomes Using Transcriptomic Data

1. Objective

This document evaluates the real-world applicability of the BlueMoon RCEv1.0 system across independent transcriptomic datasets spanning multiple diseases.

The goal is to determine whether patient-level biological structure can be used to:

  • Improve trial cohort composition
  • Identify likely non-responders
  • Enhance overall treatment response rates

Three operational use cases are tested:

  • Trial Enrichment
  • Early Failure Detection
  • Trial Exclusion

All analyses are conducted without dataset-specific retraining.

2. Dataset Overview

The evaluation includes 40+ independent datasets across:

  • Autoimmune diseases (RA, SLE, IBD, psoriasis)
  • Oncology (breast, colorectal, gastric, liver, ovarian, head & neck)
  • Neurological and psychiatric conditions (AD, PD, MS, migraine, autism, ALS)
  • Systemic conditions (sepsis, viral infection, fibromyalgia)

Datasets include a mix of:

  • Response cohorts (responder vs non-responder)
  • Case/control cohorts
  • Longitudinal datasets (pre/post treatment)

All datasets pass strict QC:

  • Minimum sample size (N ≥ 25)
  • Transcriptomic mapping to canonical gene space
  • Deduplication and leakage controls

Figure A — Dataset Distribution

3. Use Case 1 — Trial Enrichment

Objective

Determine whether ranking patients by biological alignment improves response rates.

Method

  • Patients are ranked using a fixed biological score (no retraining)
  • Top 10–50% of patients are selected
  • Response rate is computed within each selected cohort

Results

Figure 1 — Multi-Dataset Enrichment Curves

Each line represents one dataset. Median curve shown in bold.

Figure 2 — Lift Distribution

One point per dataset. Lift defined as: Top cohort response rate / baseline response rate.

Interpretation

  • Consistent increase in response rate across structured datasets
  • Magnitude varies by disease and dataset quality
  • No artificial lift observed in diffuse biological regimes
Conclusion: Patient ranking improves cohort composition prior to trial enrollment.

4. Use Case 2 — Early Failure Detection

Objective

Identify patients unlikely to respond based on biological state.

Method

  • Patients are scored based on alignment toward a biological reference state
  • Cohorts are split into:
    • Higher-alignment (favorable state)
    • Lower-alignment (unfavorable state)
  • Response rates are compared between groups

Figure 3 — Response Gap Across Datasets

Each point represents one dataset. Gap = response rate difference between groups.

Figure 4 — Example Patient-Level Ranking

Patients ranked by biological score. Responders vs non-responders overlaid.

Interpretation

  • Patients with unfavorable biological profiles show consistently lower response rates
  • Separation persists across datasets without retraining
Conclusion: Biological state can be used to identify likely non-responders prior to or early in treatment.

5. Use Case 3 — Trial Exclusion

Objective

Evaluate whether removing predicted non-responders improves trial outcomes.

Method

  • Patients ranked using the same scoring system
  • Bottom 10–30% removed
  • Response rate recomputed in remaining cohort

Figure 5 — Baseline vs Post-Exclusion (Paired Plot)

Each line represents one dataset. Shows response rate before and after exclusion.

Figure 6 — Improvement Distribution

One point per dataset. Improvement = post-exclusion rate − baseline rate.

Interpretation

  • Removing lowest-ranked patients improves response rates across structured datasets
  • Effect size varies but remains consistently positive
Conclusion: Trial outcomes can be improved without modifying treatment, solely through cohort refinement.

6. Cross-Disease Behavior

Objective

Understand how system performance varies across biological regimes.

Figure 7 — Entropy vs Signal

X-axis: latent entropy (biological complexity)
Y-axis: measurable signal (Δd or ΔdM)

Interpretation

  • Structured diseases (oncology, some autoimmune): strong directional signal, higher enrichment
  • Diffuse diseases (psychiatry, neuro): weak directional signal, persistent but lower-magnitude biological movement
Conclusion: System behavior is governed by biological structure: Prediction strength decreases with increasing entropy. Measurable biological signal persists across all regimes.

7. Limitations

  • Reduced signal strength in high-entropy diseases
  • Partial gene coverage reduces projection fidelity
  • Response-based use cases require labeled outcomes
  • Effect size varies across datasets and disease domains

8. Conclusion

Across 40+ independent datasets:

  • Patient ranking improves trial cohort composition
  • Biological state separates responders from non-responders
  • Excluding unfavorable profiles improves response rates
  • The system generalizes across diseases without retraining

9. Practical Implications

BlueMoon RCEv1.0 enables:

  • Pre-trial patient selection
  • Early identification of failing patients
  • Optimization of trial cohorts

These capabilities operate using transcriptomic data alone and do not require model retraining per dataset.

10. Appendix

The full dataset registry, QC ingestion rules, metric definitions, and additional per-dataset outputs are available in the master project repository.
